includedSources.name: The Dark Side of the Boom

items.source: All Star Charts (Alfonso De Pablos, CMT)
items.title: Equal-weight semis (XSD) breaks its March VWAP — and the INTC puts are already up 180%
items.idea: XSD is carving a short-term top and slipping below the VWAP anchored to the March lows, opening the door to a deeper corrective phase. All Star Charts bought INTC puts Monday on this exact setup; by Tuesday they were up 180%. Not a bull-market-is-over call — just a consolidation while other sectors 'pick up the slack.'
items.angle: Confirms the SixSigmaCapital and Big Moves reads elsewhere in this window — three independent sources converging on 'semis are tired' the same trading day.
items.tweetA: XSD just broke its March VWAP — the line that's held since the April lows. A CMT bought INTC puts Monday on this exact setup. Up 180% by Tuesday. When the technicals and the fundamentals (Samsung miss, memory capex questions) agree, that's not noise.
items.tweetB: Three different research shops flagged 'semis are tired' independently today — a chartist, a positioning trader, and an AI-capex analyst. That's not a coincidence, that's a regime shift nobody's tweeted about yet.

charts.desc: XSD equal-weight semiconductor ETF chart showing the breakdown below its March VWAP after the historic April-June rally.

items.title: Bitcoin's 6-month-to-10-year holder cohort MVRV is the tell before the price top — not after
items.idea: Adjusted LTH MVRV (6M-10Y cohort) strips out short-term speculation to isolate unrealized P&L for the holders who actually move supply at cycle turns. The thesis: deep bear corrections start when this profit multiple rolls over — 2-4 months BEFORE the price collapse, not at it.
items.angle: With BTC's Fear & Greed at 19 and the Coinbase premium negative for 50 straight days (see separate item), it's worth checking whether this LTH cohort metric already rolled — if so, Wintermute's 'relief rally, not a bottom' call is right for a reason nobody's citing.
items.tweetA: There's a Bitcoin metric that reportedly rolls over 2-4 months before price actually tops: the profit multiple of the 6-month-to-10-year holder cohort. Price is the lagging indicator here, not the signal.
items.tweetB: Fear & Greed at 19, Coinbase premium negative for 50 straight days, and BTC long-term-holder profit multiples said to be rolling over. Calling this a 'relief rally, not a bottom' isn't a hot take anymore — it's three separate data sets agreeing.

charts.desc: Adjusted LTH MVRV (6M-10Y cohort) chart referenced but not fully visible in the free preview — paywalled deep-dive.

items.title: Stocks gap down, oil gaps up, bonds gap down together — 9th time in 4 months, only the 14th time ever
items.idea: Since USO's 2006 launch, today is only the 14th day it's gapped up 2.5%+ while SPY gapped down 0.5%+ AND TLT gapped down too — and the 9th occurrence in just the last four months, versus zero in USO's first ten years. On these days SPY performance is a coin flip (46% win rate intraday), USO tends to fade (-0.49% median close), and TLT tends to recover (+0.21% median, positive two-thirds of the time).
items.angle: The frequency shift is the real story — a three-asset gap pattern that happened five times combined in 19 years is now happening every couple of weeks, meaning the 'buy the panic' playbook is running in a fundamentally different, more fragile regime than it did all last year.
items.tweetA: Nine of the last sixteen weeks have produced a stock/oil/bond gap combo that happened only five times total in USO's first nineteen years. The 'regime' isn't Iran. The regime is this correlation breakdown becoming routine.
items.tweetB: Everyone's trading today's Iran headline. Nobody's asking why this exact three-asset gap pattern has now fired nine times since March. That clustering is the actual regime change, not the ceasefire ending.

charts.desc: SPY chart marking all 14 prior USO-gap-up / SPY-gap-down / TLT-gap-down days, showing the cluster forming in 2025-26.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: NATO's Rutte just gave Trump 'alliance cover' for restarting the Iran war — watch for escalation by inches, not one trigger
items.idea: NATO chief Mark Rutte called the renewed US strikes 'absolutely necessary' after Iran allegedly hit international shipping, converting an isolated US decision into a NATO-blessed permission structure. The framework: this won't be one dramatic escalation, but a chain of individually-defensible 'necessary responses' that quietly rebuilds the war premium.
items.angle: This is the mechanism to watch on oil positioning — 'escalation by inches' explains why the war premium keeps grinding higher on each fresh headline instead of spiking-and-fading the way June's flare-up did.
items.tweetA: NATO didn't declare war on Iran. It did something more useful for Trump: gave him alliance cover, one 'absolutely necessary' response at a time. That's how a war premium gets rebuilt without anyone admitting the ceasefire is dead.
items.tweetB: The 'contained diplomatic wobble' framing died the moment Rutte spoke. Nobody's repricing Hormuz risk yet because everyone's still trading this like June's movie. It isn't — watch tanker insurance rates, not just oil spot.

charts.desc: No chart embedded — text analysis only. Charts inside if opened on web.

items.title: 193 perps DEXs exist — one trader runs a systematic delta-neutral funding-rate arbitrage across just three of them
items.idea: Funding-rate arbitrage across perps DEXs (long on one exchange, short on another) is generating roughly 14% APR at 2x leverage while also farming pre-TGE airdrop points. The selection filter: a real product USP, a committed token allocation (Extended has promised 30% of supply at TGE), and genuine CT mindshare density.
items.angle: 'Attention economy = token success' is a cleaner, more testable pre-TGE framework than most of crypto twitter's discourse — worth tracking Variational and Extended's eventual token launches as a live test of the thesis.
items.tweetA: 193 perps DEXs exist. One farmer's filter for which ones matter: real USP, a committed airdrop percentage, and whether anyone on CT actually talks about it. That's a tighter venture framework than most seed-stage memos.
items.tweetB: 14% APR, market-neutral, funded entirely by arbitraging funding rates between two exchanges. This is the closest thing DeFi has to an actual market-making business model, and it gets almost no attention next to the memecoin noise.

charts.desc: ATOM funding-rate spread chart between Hyperliquid and Variational over 30 days — charts inside, worth opening.

items.title: Gold is down 25% relative to every historical war-shock analog at this same point in the cycle — and still bleeding
items.idea: Dauber overlays the 2026 Iran conflict against every major oil-and-war shock since 1973 (1979, 1990, 2022, June 2025) — in every prior analog, gold was up roughly 15% by this point. This time it's down about 25% and still falling, even as oil spikes and equities sell off. That's not a safe-haven failure, it's a crowded metals long getting forcibly deleveraged underneath the actual supply shock.
items.angle: This is the sharpest counter-narrative data point in the whole window — gold's 'safe haven' reputation is being directly falsified by the tape in real time, and it's barely getting attention next to the oil and bond headlines.
items.tweetA: Gold is down 25% versus every single historical war-shock analog since 1973 at this exact point in the escalation. Not 'gold isn't working as a hedge.' Gold is getting margin-called out of a crowded long while the actual crisis unfolds underneath it.
items.tweetB: Everyone's asking why gold isn't catching a war bid. Wrong question. The right one: how much of the 2025-26 gold rally was actually a leveraged momentum trade wearing a 'safe haven' costume — and is that unwind now the bigger story than Iran itself?

charts.desc: Gold rebased to war-onset date — red line (2026 Iran) vs. blue line (average of 1973/1979/1990/2022/June 2025 shocks) — a stark divergence.

items.title: Samsung posted a RECORD Q2 profit and the stock still fell 6.3% — first AI-hardware crack that isn't just a positioning unwind
items.idea: KOSPI fell 5.35% into a confirmed bear market (over 20% off its June 19 high) after Samsung beat on profit but missed on revenue against sky-high expectations. Dauber's read: the debate just moved from 'crowded longs selling' to 'valuation finally biting,' with Micron, SanDisk and WDC already soft premarket in sympathy.
items.angle: Pairs directly with the Big Moves capex-reset piece and the Dark Side memory piece on SK Hynix's listing — three sources the same day independently flagging the memory/AI-hardware cycle as maturing, not accelerating.
items.tweetA: Samsung's Q2 was a record profit quarter and the stock still dropped 6.3%, dragging KOSPI into a bear market. When a beat gets sold, the market isn't pricing this quarter — it's pricing whether next year's quarter still justifies this multiple.
items.tweetB: Micron, SanDisk and WDC all went soft premarket on a Samsung REVENUE miss, not an earnings miss. That's the memory complex trading as one crowded basket again — exactly the setup that broke every prior memory supercycle.

charts.desc: No chart embedded in this note.

items.title: The dollar long is the most crowded since 2015 — exactly the setup that gets squeezed when the safe-haven reflex stalls
items.idea: Non-commercial specs show roughly $39.7bn net-long USD as of June 30, the most bullish since 2015, fed further by the safe-haven reflex to Iran. Meanwhile equity funds saw -$17.2bn in redemptions the week of July 1 even as markets hit record closes, and gold/silver net longs (+181.3K and +23.8K) are unwinding in real time.
items.angle: Three crowded, offsetting positioning extremes — max-long dollar, unwinding gold/silver longs, and equity outflows into record highs — resolving simultaneously is a bigger structural risk than the Iran headline itself.
items.tweetA: Dollar longs: most crowded since 2015. Gold/silver longs: unwinding in real time. Equity fund outflows: -$17.2B into record closes. Three trades that don't fit together, all resolving at once. That's the actual tail risk today, not the ceasefire.
items.tweetB: A crowd max-long the dollar right as the 'safe haven reflex' stops paying is a squeeze waiting for a trigger. Iran might just be the trigger, not the cause.

charts.desc: Positioning table: spec USD net-long, crude, ES/NQ net short, equity fund flows, gold/silver net long.

items.title: SEC's crypto safe-harbor rule could land this month — $5M raises exempt for 4 years, up to $75M/year for later-stage
items.idea: Chair Atkins' plan would let early-stage projects raise roughly $5 million over four years and larger raises up to $75 million annually, shielding tokens from securities rules once creators stop steering the project. The proposal currently sits at the White House's OIRA for review — and becomes the industry's fallback path if the CLARITY Act stalls in the Senate before August recess.
items.angle: A concrete numeric threshold ($75M annual raise cap) is a much sharper leading indicator than the usual 'regulatory clarity coming' headline — worth tracking as a signal of US-based token issuance reviving.
items.tweetA: The SEC's plan: $5M exempt raises over 4 years, up to $75M/year for bigger ones, safe harbor once founders stop steering the project. If this lands before CLARITY dies in the Senate, it becomes the entire US token-issuance rulebook by default.
items.tweetB: Congress can't pass crypto market-structure legislation, so the SEC is just going to write the rule itself via rulemaking. Watch OIRA's review status more closely than the Senate calendar.

items.title: Vanguard, the $10T holdout that called Bitcoin 'inconsistent with its long-term philosophy,' just posted its first crypto hire
items.idea: The new Head of Digital Assets role covers a multi-year roadmap across tokenization, stablecoins and custody. Vanguard refused to even let clients trade spot Bitcoin ETFs in 2024 while BlackRock, Fidelity and Franklin Templeton rushed in, and softened only last December. CEO Salim Ramji ran BlackRock's iShares and helped launch one of the first spot BTC ETFs before joining the industry's most crypto-skeptical shop.
items.angle: The CEO backstory is the real tell — Vanguard didn't soften because sentiment shifted, it softened because it hired the executive who built the competing product at BlackRock. Institutional adoption here is a personnel pipeline, not a vibe shift.
items.tweetA: Vanguard's CEO literally helped launch the first spot Bitcoin ETF at BlackRock before joining the firm that refused to let clients touch it. Eighteen months later, Vanguard is hiring a crypto chief. This was never about conviction — it's about who won the internal argument.
items.tweetB: The last crypto holdout in TradFi just staffed a role for it, with $10T in assets behind the decision. Forget today's price action — that's a bigger multi-year flow signal than anything Iran-related.

items.title: Kraken wants to become the first crypto exchange with a full European bank license, targeting Lithuania
items.idea: Following the exact playbook Revolut used in 2018 (a Lithuanian license, then EU-wide accounts, lending and stock trading), Kraken's banking arm already holds a Fed master account — a first for a crypto firm — plus a UAE authorization. Co-CEO Arjun Sethi's stated plan: collect licenses region by region over the next decade.
items.angle: A crypto-native firm methodically building a global banking-charter portfolio is the regulatory-arbitrage mirror image of what stablecoin issuers are doing — worth tracking as the 'quiet infrastructure' story underneath the price headlines.
items.tweetA: Kraken already has a Fed master account. Now it wants a European bank charter via Lithuania, using the exact playbook Revolut used to go from fintech app to EU-wide bank. Crypto exchanges aren't becoming banks reluctantly — they're doing it deliberately, jurisdiction by jurisdiction.
items.tweetB: A decade-long, region-by-region banking-license strategy is not what a company under existential regulatory threat does. It's what a company confident regulation is the moat does.

items.title: North American oil output hit 31,350 kbpd in April — the 'shale contraction' story got delayed by the Iran war rally
items.idea: North American petroleum liquids production rose +188 kbpd m/m (+1,354 kbpd y/y), reaccelerating in both the US and Canada. The author's read: what was expected to be the start of a price-driven US shale contraction got postponed because the Iran war spike let producers hedge forward and de-risk further volume growth. Continental product demand also rose +177 kbpd m/m to 25,165 kbpd.
items.angle: This is the supply-side rebuttal to every 'Hormuz shock = structural oil shortage' narrative circulating this window — US and Canadian output is still growing into the crisis, not shrinking.
items.tweetA: Everyone's pricing Hormuz risk like US shale is tapped out. April data says the opposite: North American output just reaccelerated to near-record highs, BECAUSE the war spike let producers hedge forward and lock in growth. The shock is subsidizing the supply response.
items.tweetB: The bear case for oil nobody's discussing: the same war premium spiking Brent is also what's paying US and Canadian producers to keep drilling. That's a self-defeating rally if it persists long enough.

charts.desc: Charts inside the 47-page paywalled data deck — worth opening for subscribers.

items.title: RBNZ hiked into the same session the Iran ceasefire collapsed — two hawkish shocks stacked on one calendar day
items.idea: Rosenberg's key takeaway ties the renewed US-Iran flare-up directly to a synchronized global bond selloff, noting the RBNZ's policy tightening — with an explicit threat to do more — landed the same morning as the ceasefire collapse, stacking two separate hawkish catalysts on top of each other.
items.angle: An RBNZ hike getting buried under Iran headlines is exactly the kind of stacked-catalyst day that produces outsized, hard-to-explain cross-asset moves after the fact.
items.tweetA: RBNZ hiked rates the same morning Trump declared the Iran ceasefire dead. Nobody's talking about the RBNZ side of that pairing, but it's part of why global bond yields are moving in lockstep with oil today, not just US 10s.
items.tweetB: Central-bank tightening plus geopolitical escalation landing on the same calendar day is how you get cross-asset moves that look irrational until you realize two separate hawkish shocks fired at once.

items.title: Bitcoin has traded cheaper on Coinbase than offshore for 50 straight days — the clearest weak-US-demand signal of this rally
items.idea: The Coinbase Premium has been negative for 50 consecutive days, meaning US buyers have been the marginal seller, not buyer, through the entire July 'rally.' Meanwhile stablecoin market cap (USDT+USDC) fell from $268B to $257B in two months, and Tether burned $2.5B of USDT on Ethereum on July 7 alone — its largest single burn since February.
items.angle: Three independent on-chain signals — negative Coinbase premium, shrinking stablecoin supply, record Tether burn — all point the same direction: liquidity is draining from crypto even as headline price action looks stable. That's a setup, not a floor.
items.tweetA: 50 straight days of Bitcoin trading cheaper in the US than offshore, stablecoin supply down $11B in two months, and a $2.5B single-day USDT burn. None of that shows up in the BTC price chart. All of it says liquidity is leaving, not arriving.
items.tweetB: 'Weak domestic demand' isn't a vibe here, it's 50 consecutive days of quantified premium data. Calling this a relief rally, not a bottom, isn't a hot take — it's what the plumbing has been saying for seven weeks straight.

items.title: Zcash is about to mathematically prove its shielded pool has never had a counterfeiting bug — AI compressed years of work into weeks
items.idea: After a 4-year-old soundness flaw sent ZEC down 40% in two days in June, the team's new 'Ironwood' shielded pool is nearing formal proof of no undetectable counterfeiting, with AI-assisted verification compressing what once took years into weeks. ZEC jumped 10-12% on the news, briefly reclaiming $500.
items.angle: 'AI-assisted formal verification compressing years into weeks' is the bigger story here, not the ZEC price move — a preview of how cryptographic security audits across all of crypto could get dramatically faster and cheaper.
items.tweetA: Zcash needed 4 years to even find its counterfeiting bug. AI-assisted formal verification is compressing the PROOF that it's fixed from years to weeks. That compression, not the ZEC price pump, is the actual story.
items.tweetB: Formal verification used to be the thing only the most paranoid auditors bothered with because it took forever. If AI just made it weeks instead of years, every L1's security assumptions are about to get cheaper to actually prove.

items.title: China's birth rate just hit its lowest point in over 75 years — while the PBOC keeps aggressively buying gold
items.idea: Two data points sit side by side in the same digest: China's birth rate at a 75+ year low, and continued aggressive Chinese gold buying. Read together, they sketch a state hedging a shrinking future workforce and tax base with a non-yielding, non-debasable reserve asset.
items.angle: The demographic-to-gold-reserve pipeline is a decade-scale thesis that rarely gets both data points side by side — worth building a longer thread connecting Chinese demographics, de-dollarization and gold demand.
items.tweetA: China's birth rate: lowest in 75+ years. China's gold buying: still aggressive. Put those next to each other and you get a state quietly hedging a shrinking future tax base with the one asset that doesn't need young workers to hold its value.
items.tweetB: Everyone models Chinese gold buying as de-dollarization. Maybe it's also a demographic hedge — a shrinking population needs a reserve asset that doesn't depend on GDP growth to matter.

items.title: Crusoe is reportedly in talks to raise $3B at a $30B valuation
items.idea: Crusoe — an AI-focused compute/data-center infrastructure company that started as a flared-gas-to-compute conversion play — is in talks for a fresh up-round the same day the broader semis/AI-hardware complex sold off publicly on capex-duration fears.
items.angle: A private AI-infra darling raising at a record valuation the same day public AI-infra stocks are getting repriced lower is the private/public AI valuation gap captured in a single headline.
items.tweetA: Crusoe reportedly raising at a $30B valuation the same day Samsung's earnings triggered a public AI-infra selloff. Private and public AI infrastructure valuations are telling two completely different stories on the same news cycle.
items.tweetB: $30B for a company that started as flared-gas-to-compute conversion. The AI capex story isn't slowing down in the private markets — it's only cracking where a public mark-to-market forces the question.

items.title: Broadcom just locked in its Apple custom-silicon relationship through 2031
items.idea: A multi-year extension of Broadcom's custom-chip supply deal with Apple landed the same day AI-hardware sentiment cracked broadly — a quiet vote of confidence in one specific semicap relationship even as the sector sold off.
items.angle: Worth flagging as a stock-specific offset to the broader 'AI capex duration' fear thesis — not every hyperscaler/OEM relationship is in doubt, just the ones without contractual lock-in.
items.tweetA: Broadcom-Apple custom silicon, locked through 2031, announced the same day the market panicked about AI capex duration. Long-term contracts are exactly what should get repriced UP on a capex-duration scare, not ignored.
items.tweetB: If capex duration is really the market's fear, stocks with contractually locked multi-year revenue — like this Broadcom-Apple extension — should be the safe harbor, not just collateral damage in the semis selloff.

items.title: The US government banned Anthropic's most capable model for 2.5 weeks over a jailbreak — then quietly let it back
items.idea: On June 12, Commerce ordered Anthropic to suspend Claude Fable 5 (and locked-down sibling Mythos 5) worldwide after Amazon researchers found a jailbreak that got the model to write exploit code; unable to verify every user's nationality in real time, Anthropic pulled access for everyone. Controls lifted June 30, the model returned July 1, and free access is now extended to July 12 before shifting to pay-per-token.
items.angle: A government treating a model's capability level as serious enough to warrant an export-control-style shutdown — even briefly — is a genuinely new regulatory precedent that's underplayed everywhere it's mentioned this window.
items.tweetA: A US government agency ordered a frontier AI model pulled from the entire public internet for 2.5 weeks over a single jailbreak. That's not a company PR event — that's the first real export-control-style shutdown of a consumer AI product, and nobody's pricing that precedent yet.
items.tweetB: Forget whether Fable 5 is 'too powerful.' The actual story is Anthropic couldn't verify user nationality fast enough to comply with an export order, so the fix was a global blackout. That's a preview of how AI export controls get enforced going forward: bluntly.

items.title: US 10yr yield is 10bps from its May high while oil sits 25% below its war peak — yields are decoupling from energy
items.idea: The bond bear market 'continues on' as a global phenomenon driven by debt and deficit concerns, not just oil: the 10yr JGB hit a fresh 29-year high (2.87%), the French OAT hit a 17-year high (3.90%, +11bps in a day), UK gilts rose 10bps to 4.95%, and German bunds rose 8bps to 3.07% — even as oil remains roughly 25% below its intraday war peak near $120.
items.angle: This corroborates ING's and Apollo's 'decoupling' framing elsewhere in this window — three separate sources independently flagged the same yields-decoupling-from-oil phenomenon the same day, a strong signal it's real, not noise.
items.tweetA: Oil is 25% below its war peak. The US 10yr is 10bps from its own peak. When yields stop tracking energy, the Fed's problem just flipped from a headline-inflation story to a core/fiscal story — a much stickier problem to fix with rate cuts.
items.tweetB: Japan's 10yr just hit a 29-year high. France's OAT hit a 17-year high. This isn't an Iran story or a US story — every major developed bond market is repricing sovereign risk at once, and oil is almost a distraction from it.

charts.desc: Two charts: oil (orange) vs. US 10yr yield (white); JGB and French 10yr yields at multi-decade highs.

items.title: The Pentagon is stockpiling 16,000 tons of battery-grade lithium — a $300M, five-year bet on critical-mineral scarcity
items.idea: The Defense Logistics Agency is seeking offers for almost 36 million pounds (about 16,000 tons) of battery-grade lithium carbonate over the next five years, a contract worth up to $300M, explicitly to 'reduce supply risks for critical minerals.' Boockvar frames it as direct confirmation of his broader commodity-stockpiling bull thesis.
items.angle: A specific, quantified government stockpiling volume is a much harder tradeable data point than the usual 'critical minerals matter' talking point — worth building a position thesis around for lithium and battery-materials names.
items.tweetA: The Pentagon just put out a tender for 16,000 tons of battery-grade lithium carbonate over 5 years, worth up to $300M. That's not a policy speech about critical minerals — that's a specific number you can build a position around.
items.tweetB: Government stockpiling used to mean oil in the SPR. Now it means lithium carbonate. The strategic-reserve concept is quietly expanding to cover the entire battery/EV/grid supply chain, one tender at a time.

items.title: One PM's real-time playbook for the semi unwind: 80% cash in the trading book, still DCA-ing into AI names in the investing book
items.idea: SixSigmaCapital notes only TSM, NVDA and AVGO among AI leaders have 'decent' valuation — and, ironically, are attracting the most shorts — while memory names look optically cheap but face earnings-durability questions. His response: keep slowly adding to AI names in the long-term book, but sit roughly 80% cash in the trading book because 'the environment is not easy right now.'
items.angle: A real allocator publicly splitting strategy by time horizon — patient accumulation vs. tactical cash — during the exact week three other sources are calling the semi top is a useful template for acting on the macro thesis rather than just observing it.
items.tweetA: One allocator's actual playbook this week: 80% cash in the trading book, still quietly adding to NVDA/AVGO/TSM in the long-term book. Not 'buy the dip,' not 'sell everything' — two different clocks running on the same portfolio.
items.tweetB: The only 'cheap' AI names left (TSM, NVDA, AVGO) are also the most shorted right now. That's not a value signal, that's a crowded consensus short colliding with a crowded consensus long — which one breaks first is the actual trade.

items.title: 6-month annualized core PCE just hit 4.1%, worst in three years — and it EXCLUDES the current energy spike
items.idea: Core PCE (ex food/energy) rose to 3.4% y/y in May, but the 6-month annualized run rate — which shows the current trend better — accelerated to 4.1%, the worst reading in three years, before folding in today's energy spike at all. Meanwhile the NY Fed's 1-year consumer inflation expectations jumped to a 3-year high of 3.3%, even as gasoline prices are actually falling.
items.angle: This directly contradicts the 'it's just an oil headline' framing dominating today's Iran coverage — underlying core inflation was already accelerating before the latest escalation, meaning the Fed's problem doesn't disappear even if oil fully round-trips lower.
items.tweetA: 6-month core PCE annualized: 4.1%, worst in 3 years. That number EXCLUDES today's oil spike entirely. Everyone's trading this as an Iran/oil story. The Fed's actual problem was already accelerating before a single missile flew this week.
items.tweetB: Consumer 1-year inflation expectations at a 3-year high, and gasoline prices are FALLING. If expectations rise while the usual scapegoat (gas prices) drops, that's expectations unanchoring on their own momentum — a much harder problem for Warsh than an oil shock.

charts.desc: 6-month annualized core PCE trend chart via WolfStreet — charts inside, worth opening.

items.source: The Bond Beat (Barclays via Bond Beat)
items.title: Barclays: midterm-year equity underperformance peaks in August-September, then Tech dominates for a year after
items.idea: The equity drag from midterm election uncertainty is concentrated in Aug-Sept (the max risk-premium point), not spread evenly through the year; post-midterms, Tech/Growth/Quality have outperformed in 8 of the last 9 midterm cycles as policy uncertainty falls to its four-year-cycle low. Barclays' base case is divided government — Republican president, Democratic House, Republican Senate.
items.angle: This gives a concrete calendar window (August-September 2026) for the next likely AI/tech drawdown, distinct from — and probably compounding with — the current Iran-driven selloff, a two-signal setup for timing the eventual entry.
items.tweetA: Barclays' data: midterm-year Tech underperformance peaks in August-September, then Tech leads in 8 of the last 9 post-midterm years. If you're waiting for a better entry into AI names than today's Iran selloff, that's the calendar window to watch.
items.tweetB: Two separate drags on tech are about to stack: the current Iran/capex-duration selloff, and the seasonal midterm-uncertainty drag that historically bottoms in Aug-Sept. Not a reason to panic today — a reason to size the eventual entry for two catalysts, not one.

charts.desc: No chart embedded in preview — charts inside if opened on web.

items.source: The Bond Beat (Russ Clark via Bond Beat)
items.title: A hedge-fund vet's thesis: the world just flipped from pro-capital to pro-labour, so yields surprise HIGHER from here
items.idea: Russ Clark's framework: in the old 'pro-capital' world (1994-2020), falling US yields pulled down JGB-Treasury spreads and strengthened the Yen; that mechanical relationship has broken — the Yen keeps weakening even as spreads collapse, and the BOJ has flipped from selling Yen/buying USD to selling USD/buying Yen. His conclusion: 'everything seems to be working in reverse,' so the next surprise is higher yields, the opposite of the 1990s-2000s playbook.
items.angle: This is a structural, multi-decade regime-change thesis, not an Iran-driven trade — if right, every 'yields fall from here' consensus call on Wall Street this week is fighting the wrong regime.
items.tweetA: A guy who spent a career trading Japan capital flows says the relationship just inverted: the BOJ used to sell Yen to force capital OUT. Now it's selling USD to defend the Yen. If that's a real regime flip, every 'yields fall from here' call on Wall Street this week is using a dead playbook.
items.tweetB: Pro-capital world: excess savings, falling yields, a strong Yen. Pro-labour world: capital scarcity and apparently the opposite of everything. If this thesis is right, the surprise from here isn't the Fed cutting — it's global yields breaking higher regardless of what the Fed does.

items.title: Fed watchers went from tracking doves to tracking hawks in April — futures now price a 25% chance of a 50bp December hike
items.idea: A full quarter-point hike is priced by the December FOMC, with an extra 25% probability baked in for a 50bp move, as incoming Chair Warsh leans into a singular focus on price stability.
items.angle: The magnitude here — markets pricing a real chance of a JUMBO hike, not just a hike — is understated in most other Fed coverage this window; the more extreme read among all the Fed-adjacent items today.
items.tweetA: Futures are now pricing a 25% chance of a 50bp hike in December. Not a hike — a JUMBO hike. That's a far more aggressive market read on Warsh than anything else circulating in research today.
items.tweetB: Everyone's debating whether the Fed cuts or holds. The futures market has already moved past that argument entirely and is pricing tail risk of a 2018-style overtightening mistake.

items.title: 15% of the world's helium supply just went offline overnight because of Hormuz — and there's no substitute and no stockpile
items.idea: Qatar's Ras Laffan facility produces roughly 15% of global helium, used in every fab on Earth for cooling; helium must move in $1M cryogenic containers at -269°C with only a 45-day window before product loss, making the supply chain structurally inelastic — unlike neon (the 2022 Ukraine-war scare), which had enough flexibility that a supply shock barely dented chip production.
items.angle: This is the single most concrete, quantified physical bottleneck in the entire window connecting the Iran/Hormuz escalation directly to AI hardware — a chokepoint story none of today's dozens of macro Hormuz takes mentioned.
items.tweetA: 15% of the world's helium supply comes from one Qatari facility, and it just went offline because of the Hormuz standoff. Every fab on Earth uses helium for cooling. Unlike 2022's neon scare, this molecule can't be substituted and can't be stockpiled — $1M cryo-containers, 45-day shelf life. That's the real AI-hardware supply-chain risk from Iran, not oil.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching Brent for the Hormuz story. The molecule that actually can't be substituted in a semiconductor fab is helium, and 15% of world supply just got cut off overnight. That's a chip-shortage story hiding inside an oil-shortage headline.

charts.desc: No chart — podcast transcript. Charts inside if opened on web.

items.title: Taiwan is '100% reliant' on Chinese semiconductor gas supply chains — one NF3 export restriction could shut down every Taiwanese fab
items.idea: China's roughly $120B 'Big Fund' built parallel, redundant NF3/fluorspar production capacity across competing provinces with zero central ROI discipline ('if we need 10,000 units, build 25,000'), resulting in overcapacity that now services the entire world's semiconductor gas needs. China controls 70% of global fluorspar — the feedstock for NF3, the 'janitor gas' used to clean every fab — and one Chinese producer alone makes 55,000 tonnes of NF3 a year against just 8,000 tonnes of domestic demand.
items.angle: A sharper, more specific version of the generic 'China dominates critical minerals' narrative — it names the exact molecule, the exact leverage point (a Taiwan fab shutdown), and the exact mechanism (state-directed overbuild with no economic logic, which is precisely why Western competitors can't match it).
items.tweetA: China controls 70% of world fluorspar, the feedstock for NF3 — the gas used to clean every semiconductor fab on Earth. One Chinese producer alone makes 7x its domestic demand in NF3. Taiwan is described as '100% reliant' on this supply chain. The CHIPS Act rebuilt fabs. Nobody rebuilt the gas supply underneath them.
items.tweetB: The Big Fund's actual strategy: 'if we need 10,000 units, build 25,000,' with zero ROI discipline, across competing provinces. That sounds insane by Western capital-allocation logic. It's also exactly how you corner a global supply chain nobody else is willing to overbuild.

charts.desc: No chart — podcast transcript, dense supply-chain detail.

items.title: D.E. Shaw returned $11.1B to investors in 2024 — more than any manager — and trained Jeff Bezos plus both Two Sigma founders
items.idea: Started with $28M in 1988 above a Greenwich Village bookstore, D.E. Shaw now runs $65-70B AUM; flagship fund Composite compounded 12.7% annualized net over 23 years with double-digit returns in 18 of them. The firm closed its flagship funds to new capital in 2013 and returned ALL of 2023's flagship profits to investors despite only single-digit gains that year — deliberately shrinking its own fee base. Four billionaires trace directly to its 1990s roster (Shaw, Bezos, and both Two Sigma founders) — more than the famous PayPal Mafia produced.
items.angle: The capital-discipline data point — returning ALL profits in a mediocre year, on purpose — is more interesting than the alumni trivia: a rare, testable example of a manager subordinating fee revenue to return quality, worth contrasting against any fund raising aggressively into 2026's froth.
items.tweetA: D.E. Shaw returned ALL of 2023's flagship profits to investors — in a year they only made single-digit gains. That's a firm choosing to shrink its own fee base rather than harvest fees on mediocre performance. Name another manager who's done that on purpose.
items.tweetB: Four billionaires came out of D.E. Shaw's 1990s roster — more than the PayPal Mafia produced — and almost nobody outside finance has heard of the firm. The most successful 'incubator' in modern markets was a stat-arb shop above a bookstore in the Village.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: SK Hynix's $28B US listing is a public audition for whether AI memory earnings are durable — not a victory lap
items.idea: Micron's blueprint for breaking memory's boom-bust cycle: 5-year customer agreements, cash deposits, and price bands with a ceiling AND floor — effectively selling upside volatility back to customers for a hard earnings floor. The catch: Micron's DRAM shipments rose only low-single-digits last quarter while ASPs jumped over 60%, meaning the 'magic' is still entirely pricing, not volume — and if all these long-term contracts mature simultaneously it could create the industry's own patent cliff.
items.angle: The 'selling covered calls on the boom' framing is an exact analogy for what Micron and SK Hynix are structurally doing, and the synchronized-contract-maturity risk is a specific, dateable risk almost nobody else is flagging about the memory supercycle thesis.
items.tweetA: Micron's DRAM volumes grew low single digits last quarter. Prices jumped over 60%. The entire 'AI memory supercycle' story right now is 100% a pricing story, 0% a volume story. That's a very different risk profile than investors are pricing.
items.tweetB: Micron and SK Hynix are locking in 5-year contracts with price floors — literally selling covered calls on the AI boom to buy insurance against the bust. Great for stability. Terrible if all those contracts mature the same year and create memory's own patent cliff.

items.title: Semiconductor indices up 80%+ YTD just started selling off on the SAME data (Samsung's beat) that should have confirmed the bull case
items.idea: The Philly Semiconductor Index dropped mid-to-high single digits as investors reframed Samsung's 18-19x profit jump and 'structurally tight supply into 2027' commentary as evidence of an ADVANCED cycle rather than an early one — flipping the narrative from scarcity to prospective overbuild. Rivian fell 18.1% on a $1.5-1.7B dilutive capital raise tied to DOE loan equity requirements; Generac fell 8.5% purely on sector-wide rotation, with zero company-specific negative news.
items.angle: 'Good news that gets sold' is the cleanest one-line framework in the whole window for what's happening across AI-infra today — pairs directly with the Samsung/Dauber item and the Dark Side memory piece as three converging confirmations of the same top-of-cycle read.
items.tweetA: Generac fell 8.5% today on literally zero company news except an executive promotion. That's not a stock-specific story — that's a sector getting margin-called out of every name with 'data center' in its investor deck, regardless of fundamentals.
items.tweetB: Samsung's memory commentary said supply stays tight into 2027 — a BULLISH data point that got read as bearish because the market decided 'record profit' now means 'peak,' not 'base.' When guidance this strong gets sold, the multiple was the risk, not the business.

items.title: Startups that pivot exactly once raise 2.5x more money and grow users 3.6x faster than those that never pivot
items.idea: Startup Genome's data across 3,000+ companies: the sweet spot isn't zero pivots, it's exactly one, made deliberately and on time — companies that pivot once or twice are 52% less likely to scale prematurely. CB Insights: 35% of startup deaths trace to 'no real market need,' a slower-motion version of the 19% that die from running out of cash outright.
items.angle: The specific multiplier — 2.5x capital, 3.6x user growth — is a much sharper benchmark than the usual 'pivots are good, actually' VC platitude, worth using when evaluating any founder considering a pivot right now.
items.tweetA: Startups that pivot exactly once raise 2.5x more capital and grow users 3.6x faster than ones that never pivot. The failure mode everyone fears — pivoting means admitting defeat — is actually the higher-performing path, by a specific, measured multiple.
items.tweetB: 35% of startups die from 'no market need,' which is just a slower version of the 19% that die from running out of cash. Same failure, caught at a different stage. The real skill isn't avoiding the pivot — it's catching the signal before the runway makes the decision for you.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: The dollar's Fed-hawk premium is built on reputation, not data — and could unwind fast if Warsh undercuts it
items.idea: Warsh's Sintra comments already acknowledged inflation risks subsiding, meaning today's FOMC minutes (from mid-June) are 'stale bread,' reflecting a Fed mindset that's already moved on. The OIS curve still prices roughly 30% odds of a July hike and about 40bps of hikes by March 2027 — 'a lot of hawk to carry on a perch that's starting to wobble' given softening labor data and falling gasoline prices.
items.angle: A direct, specific contrarian call against the crowded-dollar-long thesis flagged separately by Dauber — worth reading together as a bull/bear pairing on the exact same trade (long USD on Fed hawkishness) from two authors the same morning.
items.tweetA: The dollar's hawkish premium rests entirely on Warsh's REPUTATION, not on what he's actually said — he already told Sintra that inflation risks are subsiding. If CPI confirms disinflation next week and Warsh sounds balanced instead of hawkish, this crowded dollar long unwinds fast.
items.tweetB: Two authors, same morning, same trade: one says the dollar long is 'the most crowded since 2015' (bearish USD). The other says the market's Fed-hawk pricing is 'stale bread' about to get staler (also bearish USD). When independent sources converge on fading the same crowded trade, that's worth more than either alone.

items.title: TMAD's SPX levels: 7,490 support first, 7,300 long-term — watching GEX walls on semis for how far the rout goes
items.idea: Specific technical levels: SPX resistance at 7,540 / 7,560 / 7,600 (long-term), support at 7,490 / 7,433 / 7,300 (long-term); XLE is getting a strong premarket bid, which the author flags as 'not a good sign for SPX' given how directly energy strength is correlating with growth/tech weakness in this tape.
items.angle: A clean numeric level-map worth screenshotting for the next few sessions — useful regardless of which fundamental thesis (semis topping, Fed hawkishness, Iran escalation) ends up driving the next leg.
items.tweetA: 7,490 is the first line in the sand for SPX right now. Below that, 7,433, then 7,300. Above 7,540-7,560, this whole 'semis are topping' narrative gets a lot less scary. Levels don't care which of today's five narratives you believe.
items.tweetB: XLE ripping premarket while SPX chops isn't a coincidence in this tape — it's energy strength directly funded by the same Iran headlines pressuring growth and tech. Watch the ratio, not just the index.
